nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline zones of Lake Tanganyika; found among and between rocks at moderate depths, often in large groups

decorPreferences: numerous caves and crevices formed by stacked rocks
slate or smooth rounded rocks mimicking Tanganyikan littoral biotope
tight rock piles with interconnecting tunnels

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of prepared and frozen foods: high-quality sinking catfish pellets, algae wafers, frozen bloodworm, frozen brine shrimp, and daphnia. Wild fish are aufwuchs scrapers and scavengers on rocky substrates, consuming algae, invertebrates, and detritus. Feed after lights-out to ensure nocturnal feeders compete successfully. Supplement with vegetable matter (blanched courgette, spinach) and vitamin-enriched frozen foods.

goodWith: Tanganyikan cichlids (shell-dwellers such as Neolamprologus multifasciatus, Lamprologus ocellatus)
other Synodontis species of similar size
avoidWith: large aggressive cichlids that can swallow or injure small catfish
very small fish or fry (opportunistic predation risk)
fish requiring soft acidic water (incompatible water chemistry)
Congo tetras or other soft-water species (conflicting parameters)

imageCandidates.notes: Photograph by Mike Peel; 5184x3456. Group of multiple individuals (shoaling species) with cream-grey leopard-spotted bodies and the diagnostic black-and-white banded dorsal/pectoral/caudal fins clearly visible; front fish sharp and well framed. Labeled S. petricola, the trade name under which this species (S. lucipinnis) is nearly always sold.

Photograph by FakirNL; 3812x2937. Two fish in a rocky-cave, sandy-bottom Tanganyikan biotope showing spotted bodies and banded fins; good habitat context. Labeled S. petricola (trade synonym for S. lucipinnis).

Photograph by Nerezza; 655x425 (low res). Clean full side profile of a single fish clearly showing the leopard spotting and black-and-white banded fins; best diagnostic reference view but small. Labeled S. petricola (trade synonym for S. lucipinnis).

summary: The Dwarf Petricola is a small, active Lake Tanganyika catfish that, despite being formally described as Synodontis lucipinnis in 2006, is nearly always sold under the name Synodontis petricola. It reaches around 9 cm and requires the hard, alkaline water chemistry typical of Lake Tanganyika. Best kept in groups of three or more.
careNotes:
The Dwarf Petricola (Synodontis lucipinnis) was formally described by Wright and Bailey in 2006 and is the species nearly always sold as 'Petricola Catfish' or 'Synodontis petricola' in the aquarium trade. The true S. petricola is extremely rare in commerce. S. lucipinnis is smaller — reaching around 9 cm — and is arguably better suited to home aquaria than the larger S. petricola, which can reach around 14 cm. Both species share the same Lake Tanganyika rocky-shoreline origin and essentially identical care requirements.

Water chemistry is the critical constraint. Lake Tanganyika is highly alkaline and hard (pH 8.5–9.0, GH 10–20, KH 10–18), and S. lucipinnis will not thrive in the neutral or soft water that many popular catfish tolerate. Decor should replicate the rocky shoreline: stacked flat rocks, slate overhangs, and interconnecting caves give the fish the retreats they naturally seek. A group of at least three fish — six or more is often recommended — will display more natural social behaviour and tends to reduce tension between individuals.

As a scaleless catfish, S. lucipinnis is highly sensitive to copper-based medications; avoid any proprietary copper treatments in tanks where this species is kept, and dose the full tank volume rather than an isolation vessel. Excellent oxygenation is important: Lake Tanganyika is a deep, clear lake with very high dissolved oxygen levels, and the fish does best in well-aerated water. Feeding should be scheduled after lights-out to suit the species' nocturnal habits; sinking pellets and wafers ensure the food reaches the bottom before cichlid tankmates can intercept it. S. lucipinnis has been bred in captivity in larger, well-established tanks.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been achieved in captivity, though it is not routine. The Dwarf Petricola is an egg-scattering spawner: eggs are deposited among rock crevices and no parental care is provided. Adults will consume eggs if they are not removed promptly. A large cave or breeding tile is sometimes used as a spawning site. Raising fry requires a separate rearing tank; newly hatched fry accept infusoria or finely powdered fry food, graduating to micro-pellets as they grow. Sexual maturity is generally reached at around 18 to 24 months. Keeping two females per male is often advised to reduce stress between individuals.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis lucipinnis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 9 cm), temperature 23–27°C, pH 8.0–9.0, hardness 10–20°H, native range (Lake Tanganyika), habitat (rocky littoral), diet, compatibility, and breeding notes. Confirmed species identity issue: virtually all trade ‘S. petricola’ are S. lucipinnis.

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis lucipinnis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), native range (Lake Tanganyika basin: DRC, Tanzania, Zambia), original description year (Wright & Bailey 2006). Confirmed temperature range and pH tolerance.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Synodontis care overview; confirmed omnivorous diet, sinking-food feeding strategy, group-keeping recommendation, and copper sensitivity for scaleless catfish. Supplemented hobbyist guidance on tank size and tankmate selection.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis lucipinnis species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed species-splitting history (described 2006; previously sold as S. petricola), adult size ~9 cm vs S. petricola ~14 cm, Tanganyikan hard-alkaline water requirements, nocturnal feeding, and group-keeping of 3+ individuals.

additional.name: Wright, J.J. & Bailey, R.M. (2006) — Systematic revision of the formerly monotypic genus Synodontis (Mochokidae) from Lake Tanganyika, via FishBase reference
additional.notes: Original taxonomic description reference; confirms formal description of S. lucipinnis in 2006, separating it from S. petricola based on morphometric and meristic characters including smaller adult size and fin-ray counts.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner. Breeding has been achieved in captivity but is not routine. Eggs are deposited in rock crevices and adults will consume them if not removed. Fry require a separate rearing tank with appropriately fine foods. Maturity is generally reached at around 18 to 24 months.
